news_summary: Belugas have demonstrated self-awareness by recognizing themselves in mirrors, joining a select group of animals with this cognitive ability.
news_summary_fr: Les bélugas ont démontré qu'ils avaient conscience d'eux-mêmes en se reconnaissant dans un miroir, rejoignant ainsi un groupe restreint d'animaux dotés de cette capacité cognitive.
news_summary_es: Las belugas han demostrado tener conciencia de sí mismas al reconocerse en un espejo, lo que las sitúa en el selecto grupo de animales que poseen esta capacidad cognitiva.

articles.title: Belugas join the short list of animals who know they’re looking at themselves
articles.summary:
There’s a short list of animals who, when placed in front of a mirror, eventually figure out they’re looking at themselves. Chimpanzees. Bottlenose dolphins. Asian elephants. A magpie or two. A small reef fish called the cleaner wrasse, which upended some assumptions about brain size when it passed the test in 2023. And now, belugas. […]
The post Belugas join the short list of animals who know they’re looking at themselves first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

There’s a short list of animals who, when placed in front of a mirror, eventually figure out they’re looking at themselves. Chimpanzees. Bottlenose dolphins. Asian elephants. A magpie or two. A small reef fish called the cleaner wrasse, which upended some assumptions about brain size when it passed the test in 2023. And now, belugas.

New findings in PLOS One document two beluga whales at the New York Aquarium doing exactly what animals on that list tend to do: testing whether the reflection responds to them, then using the mirror to examine themselves. One of the whales, Natasha, went on to pass what researchers call the mark test, where a mark is drawn somewhere on the animal’s body that it can’t see without a mirror. The footage was shot in 2001, yet by some twist of fate, it took until now to be published.

The footage that almost wasn’t

Diana Reiss, a marine mammal scientist at Hunter College, had already documented mirror self-recognition in bottlenose dolphins and Asian elephants. In 2001, she set up the same test for four captive belugas: three adult females and a seven-year-old named Maris. A mirror was affixed to a window in the whales’ shared pool for two-hour sessions. The team filmed the results and also filmed the whales’ responses to clear plexiglass placed in the same spot, as a control.

Then life moved on. “We kind of let it go,” Reiss says. The footage sat for nearly two decades.

In 2020, Alexander Mildener enrolled in Reiss’s program. He had grown up visiting the New York Aquarium and described being “completely transfixed” by those exact belugas as a child. With the pandemic limiting field research, he needed a thesis project. Reiss pulled out the tapes. Mildener spent lockdown carefully watching videos of his old friends, “the very whales that inspired me to be in this field in the first place,” he says.

What Natasha and Maris did

Animals that go on to recognize themselves in mirrors tend to follow a recognizable sequence. First, a reaction to the mirror as a new object or a potential peer. Then, contingency testing: repetitive movements designed to check whether the image is responding to them. Reiss compares this to how people behave near security monitors in a store. When you think you see yourself on the screen, “you might move your head or raise your hand: ‘Is that me?’” For animals who pass, “that seems to be where the light bulb goes on.”

Natasha and Maris went through the full sequence. In their first session, both clapped their jaws at their own reflections, a behavior belugas use for intimidation with each other. Then they started testing: Natasha nodded at the mirror, while Maris waggled her head in multiple directions. By their second session, both were using the mirror to watch themselves barrel-roll and look inside their own mouths. Maris reared up and flapped her pectoral fins at the glass in a move researchers named a “pec shimmy.” Both whales blew bubbles and then bit them, behaviors that don’t appear when the mirror isn’t present. “It was just really beautiful to watch,” Mildener says.

Natasha passed the mark test. Maris did not, which is consistent with patterns seen across many mirror-recognition studies, where some individuals within the same species pass while others don’t.

Why the list keeps growing

Each addition to the mirror self-recognition list chips away at a prior assumption. “We have this list of the things that only humans do,” Reiss says, “and over time we’ve been checking them off.” The cleaner wrasse finding in 2023 was particularly disruptive: it suggested that large, sophisticated brains aren’t necessarily the threshold researchers assumed they were. What does seem consistent is that species that pass tend to be highly social and capable of recognizing individuals of their own kind.

The stakes aren’t only scientific. When humpback whale behavioral research expanded in the 1970s, it helped build public support for the Endangered Species Act and the Marine Mammal Protection Act. Reiss sees the same potential in this kind of work. “Finding these shared capabilities and shared levels of consciousness and self-awareness in other species seem to engender more empathy for them,” she says.

Mildener and Reiss hope to run similar tests with other beluga populations. For Mildener, who spent lockdown watching the animals that had first drawn him to marine science, the project carried a particular weight. The whales that set him on his path were also the ones who gave him his first major finding.

Source study: PLOS One- Evidence for mirror self-recognition in beluga whales (Delphinapterus leucas)

articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that beluga whales demonstrate mirror self-recognition, a sign of self-awareness previously confirmed in only a few animal species. This finding challenges assumptions about animal cognition and has broad implications for empathy and conservation efforts, benefiting society's understanding of animal intelligence and welfare.
articles.personality_title: Beluga whales pass mirror self-recognition test, joining a rare group of self-aware animals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have a special test called the mirror self-recognition test. It shows if animals understand that their reflection in a mirror is actually themselves. Only a few animals, like chimpanzees, dolphins, and elephants, have passed this test so far.

**What happened** – New research showed that beluga whales can also recognize themselves in mirrors. Two belugas at the New York Aquarium were watched in 2001 using a mirror placed in their pool. One whale named Natasha passed the test by noticing a mark placed on her body that she could only see in the mirror. The other whale, Maris, showed many signs of understanding the mirror but did not pass the mark test. The videos were studied and published many years after they were recorded.

**Impact** – This discovery adds beluga whales to the small list of animals known to have this self-awareness. It challenges old ideas that only animals with big brains can pass the test. Instead, it seems animals that live closely with others and recognize each other are more likely to pass. This kind of knowledge can help people feel more connected to these animals and support their protection.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to test more belugas from different places to see if this ability is common among them. They hope this will teach us even more about how smart whales are and help improve how we care for and protect them.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Beluga whales have shown they can recognize themselves in mirrors, joining a rare group of animals with this self-awareness and changing how we understand animal intelligence.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les bélugas reconnaissent leur reflet dans un miroir, rejoignant un groupe rare d’animaux conscients d’eux-mêmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent un test spécial appelé le test de reconnaissance de soi dans un miroir. Il montre si les animaux comprennent que leur reflet est en fait eux-mêmes. Seuls quelques animaux, comme les chimpanzés, les dauphins et les éléphants, ont réussi ce test jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle recherche a montré que les bélugas peuvent aussi se reconnaître dans un miroir. Deux bélugas de l’Aquarium de New York ont été observés en 2001 avec un miroir placé dans leur bassin. Une baleine nommée Natasha a réussi le test en remarquant une marque sur son corps visible seulement dans le miroir. L’autre, Maris, a montré des signes de compréhension sans réussir le test de la marque. Les vidéos ont été analysées et publiées des années plus tard.

**Impact** – Cette découverte ajoute les bélugas à la courte liste d’animaux ayant cette conscience de soi. Elle remet en question l’idée que seuls les animaux avec un gros cerveau peuvent réussir. Il semble plutôt que les espèces sociales qui se reconnaissent entre elles ont plus de chances d’y arriver. Cela peut aider les gens à mieux comprendre et protéger ces animaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester d’autres bélugas de différents endroits pour savoir si cette capacité est commune. Ils espèrent mieux comprendre l’intelligence des baleines et améliorer leur protection.

**En une phrase** – Les bélugas ont montré qu’ils peuvent se reconnaître dans un miroir, rejoignant un groupe rare d’animaux conscients d’eux-mêmes et changeant notre vision de l’intelligence animale.
articles.personality_title_es: Las belugas reconocen su reflejo en un espejo, uniéndose a un grupo raro de animales autoconscientes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan una prueba llamada test de autorreconocimiento en espejo para saber si los animales entienden que su reflejo es ellos mismos. Solo unos pocos animales, como chimpancés, delfines y elefantes, han pasado esta prueba.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio mostró que las belugas también pueden reconocerse en un espejo. Dos belugas en el Acuario de Nueva York fueron observadas en 2001 con un espejo en su piscina. Una beluga llamada Natasha pasó la prueba al notar una marca en su cuerpo que solo podía ver en el espejo. La otra, Maris, mostró señales de entender el espejo pero no pasó la prueba de la marca. Los videos fueron estudiados y publicados muchos años después.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo añade a las belugas a la pequeña lista de animales con esta autoconciencia. Reta la idea antigua de que solo los animales con cerebros grandes pueden pasar la prueba. Parece que los animales sociales que se reconocen entre sí tienen más posibilidades. Esto puede ayudar a que las personas se conecten más con estos animales y apoyen su protección.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar a más belugas de diferentes lugares para ver si esta habilidad es común. Esperan aprender más sobre la inteligencia de las ballenas y mejorar su cuidado y protección.

**Una frase clave** – Las belugas han demostrado que pueden reconocerse en espejos, sumándose a un grupo raro de animales con autoconciencia y cambiando nuestra comprensión de la inteligencia animal.
articles.image_prompt: Two gentle beluga whales curiously examining their reflections in a calm, clear pool with a large mirror, softly illuminated by natural light, rendered in warm, muted tones with delicate brushstrokes capturing their playful and introspective expressions.
